context_lines: In **Matthew 18**, Jesus teaches His disciples about humility, stumbling, restoration, forgiveness, and life together in His kingdom. Its language about agreement, witnesses, and gathered presence belongs to this communal and disciplinary context, not to isolated proof-texting.
explanation_lines: *Read the full passage — Matthew 18:15-20.* Jesus is not teaching on prayer. He is teaching on church discipline — specifically, the process for addressing sin between believers. Verse 15: go to your brother alone. Verse 16: take two or three witnesses. Verse 17: bring it before the church. Verse 18: whatever you bind or loose on earth will be bound or loosed in heaven. Verse 19 follows directly from that sequence — the "agreeing" is the gathered church making a unified judgment in a matter of discipline, not two friends agreeing on a prayer request.

The "two or three" language is a direct echo of Deuteronomy 19:15 — *"A single witness shall not rise up against a man on account of any iniquity or any sin... on the evidence of two or three witnesses a matter shall be confirmed."* Jesus is applying the Old Testament standard for legal testimony to the process of church discipline. He is not establishing a prayer formula.

"Where two or three have gathered together in My name, I am there in their midst" is similarly misread. It is not saying that small prayer groups have a special power unavailable to individuals. It is saying that when the church gathers in His name to exercise the authority He has given it — including the difficult work of discipline and restoration — He is present in that process, authorizing and confirming what is done faithfully.
